intent: Same source domain and same target TYPE (shadow_t) but TWO different classes: file{read} and dir{search}. Tests that perms are NOT unioned across different classes (f4 contract #5) - two separate allow lines, one per (sdomain, ttype, tclass) triple.
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay); floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic).

oracle.audit2allow_N: allow logrotate_t shadow_t:dir search; allow logrotate_t shadow_t:file read;
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: -R keeps the raw dir:search allow (no interface matched it) but converts the file:read denial into the auth_read_shadow() macro (attribute + read_file_perms 5-perm padding + etc_t:dir 6-perm grant). Our expected.te keeps BOTH as raw allows: file:read and dir:search on separate lines, no macro, no cross-class union, no padding.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A naive grouper keyed only on (sdomain, ttype) - forgetting tclass - would wrongly union file:read and dir:search into one rule like `allow logrotate_t shadow_t:??? { read search };` (read is not a valid dir perm, search is not a valid file perm - a broken/uncompilable rule). The class MUST be part of the grouping key.
